title: Port VcStore read/query methods to fsqlite
description:
Port all VcStore methods that SELECT/read data from the database (42+ methods).

## Method Categories

### Fleet & Health Queries (6 methods)
- get_freshness_summaries() — query_map with JOIN-like logic
- list_collector_health() — query_map with optional filters
- get_machine_baseline() — query_row with QueryReturnedNoRows
- list_machine_baselines() — standard query
- list_drift_events() — dynamic WHERE conditions
- check_drift() — computation-heavy, reads baseline then compares

### Audit & Events (3 methods)
- list_audit_events() — dynamic WHERE with AuditEventFilter
- get_audit_event() — query_row with QueryReturnedNoRows handling
- list_vacuum_history() — standard query

### Retention Policies (2 methods)
- list_retention_policies() — query_map with complex row extraction
- get_retention_policy() — query_row with QueryReturnedNoRows

### Delivery & Decisions (4 methods)
- list_delivery_logs() — query_map with dynamic Vec<Box<dyn duckdb::ToSql>>
- delivery_summary() — query_map aggregation
- list_autopilot_decisions() — query_map with dynamic Vec<Box<dyn duckdb::ToSql>>
- autopilot_decision_summary() — query_map aggregation

### Incidents (5 methods)
- get_incident() — **uses to_json() in SQL**, QueryReturnedNoRows
- list_incidents() — **uses to_json() in SQL**, dynamic Vec<Box<dyn duckdb::ToSql>>
- get_incident_notes() — **uses to_json() in SQL**
- get_incident_timeline() — **uses to_json() in SQL**
- list_replay_snapshots() — standard query

### Fleet Commands & Sessions (5 methods)
- list_fleet_commands() — **uses to_json() in SQL**, dynamic Vec<Box<dyn duckdb::ToSql>>
- list_unmined_sessions() — **uses to_json() in SQL**
- mining_stats() — **uses to_json() in SQL**
- list_poll_decisions() — standard query
- list_profile_samples() — standard query

### Data Export (4 methods)
- list_tables() — catalog query
- export_table_jsonl() — **uses to_json() in SQL** for row serialization
- table_row_count() — query_scalar
- build_export_manifest() — orchestration method

### Other Reads (13 methods)
- get_cursor() — query_row with QueryReturnedNoRows
- query_scalar<T>() — generic scalar query
- list_routing_events() — standard query
- routing_event_summary() — aggregation query
- list_resolutions() — standard query
- count_resolutions_by_type() — query_row aggregation
- distinct_resolution_alert_types() — query_map
- get_digest_report() — standard query
- list_digest_reports() — standard query
- list_redaction_events() — standard query
- redaction_summary() — standard query
- list_playbook_drafts() — standard query
- get_playbook_draft() — **uses to_json()**, QueryReturnedNoRows

## Key Porting Patterns

### to_json() Replacement (11 methods + query_json wrapper)
DuckDB pattern: SELECT to_json(_row) FROM (...) AS _row (lines 1792-3285)
These methods embed DuckDB's to_json() for JSON serialization in SQL.
FrankenSQLite has no to_json(). Two replacement strategies:
a) Query raw columns, construct JSON in Rust with serde_json (preferred)
b) Use json_object() / json_array() from json1 extension
c) For export_table_jsonl: must reconstruct row-level JSON in Rust

Note: The query_json() wrapper at line 518 also uses to_json() — this is
ported in bd-zut, and fixing it automatically fixes all callers that go
through query_json(). The 11 methods here have THEIR OWN to_json() SQL
that must each be individually ported.

### QueryReturnedNoRows (7 locations)
Lines 577, 777, 1217, 1805, 2376, 2508, 3298
Pattern: match Err(duckdb::Error::QueryReturnedNoRows) => Ok(None)
FrankenSQLite: match Err(FrankenError::QueryReturnedNoRows) => Ok(None)

### Dynamic Parameters (Vec<Box<dyn duckdb::ToSql>>, 7 locations)
Lines 431, 486, 1471, 1608, 1733, 1886, 3414
Used for conditional WHERE clauses with nullable filters.
FrankenSQLite: Vec<SqliteValue> — simpler, no trait objects needed.

### query_map Pattern (13 methods)
DuckDB: stmt.query_map(params, |row| { ... }).collect()
FrankenSQLite: Equivalent row iteration with SqliteValue extraction.

## Row Extraction Helpers
Create utilities for common SqliteValue → Rust type patterns:
- fn extract_string(row, idx) -> Option<String>
- fn extract_i64(row, idx) -> Option<i64>
- fn extract_f64(row, idx) -> Option<f64>
- fn extract_bool(row, idx) -> Option<bool> (SqliteValue::Integer → bool)
- fn extract_json(row, idx) -> Option<serde_json::Value>
- fn extract_timestamp(row, idx) -> Option<String>

## Testing
- Unit test each row extraction helper with all SqliteValue variants
- Test each to_json() replacement produces identical JSON output
- Test QueryReturnedNoRows handling for all 7 locations
- Test dynamic parameter construction for variable-length queries
- Test query_map iteration with empty results, single row, multiple rows
- Integration test: round-trip insert→query for each method category
- Log: method name, row count, JSON shape, test status

## Files Changed
- crates/vc_store/src/lib.rs (42+ methods, row extraction helpers)

title: Phase 1: Replace tokio dependencies and scaffold Asupersync runtime
notes:
Background: The project must drop Tokio completely in favor of /dp/asupersync. This is a foundational shift to structured concurrency using explicit 'Cx' capability tokens and region-based lifecycles. We must preserve all runtime capabilities (like thread pools and timers) without losing performance or safety.

Tasks:
1. Modify Cargo.toml workspace and all child crates to remove tokio, tokio-util, and tokio-stream, replacing them with asupersync (path = '/dp/asupersync') and its compatibility layers (e.g. asupersync-tokio-compat) where absolutely necessary for third-party crates.
2. Replace #[tokio::main] in vc_cli/src/main.rs with an explicit Asupersync runtime initialization (e.g., asupersync::runtime::Runtime::new().block_on(...)).
3. Establish the root Region and Cx capability token in main.rs, passing them down to all sub-components (collectors, alert dispatcher, web server, etc.) to enforce strict structured concurrency.

Testing & Validation:
- Unit Tests: Write startup/shutdown tests validating the Asupersync runtime initializes and tears down cleanly without dangling tasks.
- E2E Tests: Implement a bash-based e2e script (e2e_runtime_boot.sh) that launches vc_cli, asserts it binds to its default ports, and gracefully terminates upon receiving a SIGTERM.
- Logging: Inject exhaustive tracing::info! and tracing::debug! spans around the Asupersync runtime setup, tracking thread pool initialization, root Region creation, and graceful shutdown sequencing. Do not lose any existing initialization logs.
close_reason: Phase 1 complete: main.rs migrated to Asupersync RuntimeBuilder + block_on with Tokio compat bridge. Root Cx via Cx::for_request(). Tokio added to workspace deps for compat. E2E runtime boot test added. Build verification blocked by worker OOM (known infra issue).

title: Phase 2c: Migrate vc_web to Asupersync HTTP stack
notes:
Background: vc_web currently relies on Axum, hyper, and tokio::net::TcpListener for its HTTP server and WebSockets (tungstenite).

- E2E Tests: Add e2e_web_endpoints.sh hitting every API route. Add e2e_web_sockets.sh to connect a WS client, assert live telemetry is streamed correctly, and verify connection stability under load.

title: Phase 6: Intelligence - Advanced Capabilities
description:
# Phase 6: Intelligence - Advanced Capabilities

## Purpose
Implement the advanced intelligence features: solution mining, playbooks, A/B testing, evolutionary optimization, natural language interface, and MCP server mode. This phase realizes the full "autonomous agent empire command center" vision.

## Key Deliverables
1. **Solution Mining**: Extract successful patterns from cass session data
2. **Playbook Generation**: Create reusable operational procedures from experience
3. **A/B Testing Framework**: Controlled experiments for agent configurations
4. **Evolutionary Optimization**: Genetic algorithm for config optimization
5. **Natural Language Interface**: Query system state with plain English
6. **MCP Server Mode**: vc as an MCP server for agent queries

## Success Criteria
- Solutions mined and stored from past sessions
- Playbooks track execution success rates
- A/B tests run with statistical significance tracking
- Evolutionary optimizer discovers improved configurations
- Natural language queries return accurate, actionable answers
- Agents can query vc via MCP tools

## Technical Context

### Solution Mining Architecture
```rust
pub struct SolutionMiner {
    pub async fn mine_solutions(&self) -> Vec<MinedSolution> {
        // Find successful sessions
        // Extract key decision points
        // Identify success factors
        // Generalize into reusable patterns
    }
}

pub struct MinedSolution {
    pub problem_pattern: ProblemSignature,
    pub solution_steps: Vec<Step>,
    pub success_rate: f64,
    pub applicable_contexts: Vec<Context>,
    pub confidence: f64,
}
```

### Gotcha Database (from Phase 5)
```rust
pub struct Gotcha {
    pub title: String,
    pub triggers: Vec<GotchaTrigger>,
    pub failure_pattern: FailurePattern,
    pub prevention: String,
    pub fix: String,
    pub occurrences: u32,
}
```

Gotchas are surfaced proactively when triggers match current context.

### A/B Testing Framework
```rust
pub struct Experiment {
    pub hypothesis: String,
    pub control: ExperimentArm,
    pub treatment: ExperimentArm,
    pub primary_metric: MetricId,
    pub stop_conditions: Vec<StopCondition>,
    pub auto_rollback: bool,
}
```

Run controlled comparisons of agent configurations with automatic statistical analysis.

### Evolutionary Optimization
```rust
pub struct EvolutionaryOptimizer {
    pub population: Vec<AgentConfig>,
    pub fitness: Box<dyn Fn(&AgentConfig, &PerformanceData) -> f64>,
    
    pub fn evolve(&mut self) -> Vec<AgentConfig> {
        // Tournament selection
        // Crossover
        // Mutation
        // Evaluate fitness
        // Return top performers
    }
}
```

Over generations, discover optimal agent configurations for specific task types.

### Natural Language Interface
Map intents to predefined queries:
- "Why is orko slow?" -> cpu + rate limit + stuck agent analysis
- "Which account should I use?" -> Oracle recommendations
- "What's blocking progress?" -> bv triage summary

Avoid free-form SQL generation until strict guardrails exist.

### MCP Server Mode
vc exposes tools for agent queries:
```rust
vc_fleet_status { ... }        // Fleet health overview
vc_find_capacity { ... }       // Available agents for work
vc_request_resources { ... }   // Resource allocation
vc_report_completion { ... }   // Task completion metrics
vc_get_recommendations { ... } // AI-powered suggestions
```

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 5 (web dashboard, autopilot, knowledge base foundation)

## Estimated Scope
- ~3-4 weeks
- Final milestone: full autonomous operation capability

## Testing & E2E Expectations
- Every deliverable in this phase must add unit tests per bd-1x9.
- Extend bd-30z with phase-specific e2e scenarios and structured logs.
- Phase completion requires `cargo test --workspace` + phase e2e suite with JSON summaries in tests/logs/.
close_reason: done

title: Implement A/B testing framework for agent configurations
Implement evolutionary optimization for agent configs
description:
## Overview
Build evolutionary optimization that automatically evolves agent configurations toward better performance using genetic algorithms.

## Background
Manual tuning of agent configurations is time-consuming. Evolutionary optimization uses genetic algorithms to automatically discover high-performing configurations.

## Algorithm
1. Initialize - Create random population of configs
2. Evaluate - Score each config on fitness metrics
3. Select - Choose top performers via tournament
4. Crossover - Combine traits from parents
5. Mutate - Random variations
6. Repeat - Until convergence or generation limit

## Configuration Genome
- genes: HashMap of configurable parameters
- Float genes (temperature, etc.)
- Int genes (max_tokens, retry_count, timeout)
- Bool genes (streaming, etc.)
- Choice genes (model selection)
- fitness: Evaluated score

## Fitness Function
Weighted combination of:
- task_success_rate (positive weight)
- avg_tokens_per_task (negative weight - lower is better)
- avg_response_time (negative weight)
- error_rate (negative weight)
- cost_per_task (negative weight)

## Genetic Operations
- Crossover: Uniform crossover from two parents
- Mutation: Gaussian mutation for floats, random flip for bools
- Elitism: Top N preserved unchanged each generation
- Tournament selection for parent choice

## Evolution Manager
- Tracks population across generations
- Runs fitness evaluation
- Applies genetic operations
- Detects convergence
- Exports best configuration

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Configuration genome with typed genes
- [ ] Fitness function combining multiple metrics
- [ ] Crossover combines parent configurations
- [ ] Mutation introduces random variations
- [ ] Tournament selection for parent choice
- [ ] Elitism preserves top performers
- [ ] Convergence detection stops evolution
- [ ] Best configuration exported for use
- [ ] Evolution history tracked and visualized

## Testing & Logging
- Deterministic unit tests with fixed seeds/time where applicable.
- Regression tests against fixture datasets.
- Safety tests: no side effects without allowlist/approval.
- E2E: scenario scripts under tests/e2e/system with JSON summaries and log artifacts.

title: Implement natural language query interface
description:
## Overview
Build a natural language interface that allows users to query vibe_cockpit data using plain English instead of SQL or specific commands.

## Background
Not all users are comfortable with SQL or CLI commands. A natural language interface democratizes access to monitoring data by translating questions like 'Which agent used the most tokens today?' into appropriate queries.

## Architecture
1. User asks question in natural language
2. LLM translates to SQL or internal query
3. Query executed against DuckDB
4. Results formatted and returned
5. Optional: explain the query that was run

## Query Translation
- Parse user intent from natural language
- Map entities (agents, accounts, machines) to tables
- Generate appropriate SQL or API calls
- Handle ambiguity with clarifying questions

## Example Queries
- 'Which agent used the most tokens today?' -> SELECT query on caut_usage
- 'Are any machines offline?' -> SELECT query on machines table
- 'Show me the rate limit status' -> Query caam_accounts
- 'What errors happened in the last hour?' -> Query dcg_events
- 'How is rch cache performance?' -> Query rch_compilations with aggregation

## Safety Considerations
- Only allow SELECT queries (no mutations)
- Validate generated SQL before execution
- Rate limit queries to prevent abuse
- Log all NL queries for audit

## Implementation Components
- Intent classifier (what type of query?)
- Entity extractor (which tables/columns?)
- SQL generator (produce valid DuckDB SQL)
- Result formatter (human-readable output)
- Ambiguity resolver (ask clarifying questions)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] NL questions translated to SQL
- [ ] Common monitoring questions handled
- [ ] Entity recognition for agents, machines, accounts
- [ ] Time expressions parsed (today, last hour, etc.)
- [ ] Aggregations supported (most, average, count)
- [ ] Query explanation available
- [ ] Read-only safety enforced
- [ ] Graceful handling of ambiguous queries

## Testing & Logging
- Deterministic unit tests with fixed seeds/time where applicable.
- Regression tests against fixture datasets.
- Safety tests: no side effects without allowlist/approval.
- E2E: scenario scripts under tests/e2e/system with JSON summaries and log artifacts.
close_reason: done

title: Implement MCP server for external access (vc_mcp crate)
description:
## Overview
Build an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that exposes vibe_cockpit data and actions to external AI agents.

## Background
Other AI agents may need to query vc data or trigger actions. An MCP server provides a standardized interface for external access, enabling agent-to-agent coordination through vibe_cockpit.

## MCP Tools to Expose

### Query Tools
- vc_query_machines: List machines with optional filters
- vc_query_accounts: Get account status and usage
- vc_query_alerts: List active and recent alerts
- vc_query_sessions: Search session history
- vc_query_metrics: Get specific metric values
- vc_query_nl: Natural language query interface

### Action Tools
- vc_acknowledge_alert: Acknowledge an alert
- vc_approve_playbook: Approve pending Guardian playbook
- vc_switch_account: Trigger account switch
- vc_probe_machine: Probe a machine for tools
- vc_refresh_collector: Force a collector to run

### Resource Tools
- vc_get_dashboard: Get current dashboard state
- vc_get_machine_detail: Get detailed machine info
- vc_get_agent_dna: Get agent DNA fingerprint

## Implementation
- Use MCP SDK for server implementation
- Connect to VcStore for data access
- Validate permissions for actions
- Rate limit external requests
- Full audit logging

## Security Considerations
- Authentication required for action tools
- Query tools may be read-only accessible
- Rate limiting per client
- Audit log of all MCP calls
- Configurable access control

## Configuration
Allow enabling/disabling specific tools:
[mcp]
enabled = true
port = 3002
allowed_tools = [query_*, vc_acknowledge_alert]
require_auth = true

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] MCP server runs on configurable port
- [ ] Query tools return appropriate data
- [ ] Action tools execute with proper validation
- [ ] Authentication enforced for sensitive actions
- [ ] Rate limiting prevents abuse
- [ ] Audit log captures all requests
- [ ] Error responses follow MCP spec
- [ ] Health check endpoint available

## Testing & Logging
- Deterministic unit tests with fixed seeds/time where applicable.
- Regression tests against fixture datasets.
- Safety tests: no side effects without allowlist/approval.
- E2E: scenario scripts under tests/e2e/system with JSON summaries and log artifacts.
close_reason: done

title: Implement audit trail system
description:
Implement comprehensive audit trail system.

PURPOSE:
Track all actions taken by vc, collectors, autopilot, and users for accountability, debugging, and compliance.

DATA MODEL (from plan Section 9.4):
CREATE TABLE audit_events(
    ts TIMESTAMP,
    event_type TEXT,  -- collector_run, autopilot_action, user_command, guardian_action
    actor TEXT,       -- collector name, autopilot, user, guardian
    machine_id TEXT,
    action TEXT,
    result TEXT,      -- success, failure, skipped
    details_json TEXT
);

CREATE INDEX idx_audit_ts ON audit_events(ts);
CREATE INDEX idx_audit_type ON audit_events(event_type);
CREATE INDEX idx_audit_machine ON audit_events(machine_id);

AUDIT EVENT TYPES:

1. COLLECTOR RUNS
- Collector name, machine_id, start/end time, duration
- Bytes parsed, rows inserted, errors/warnings
- Cursor state changes

2. AUTOPILOT EXECUTIONS
- Exact command run
- Machine_id
- User confirmation state (if applicable)
- Result exit code
- Before/after state summary

3. USER COMMANDS
- All vc CLI invocations
- TUI/web interactions that trigger actions
- Parameters and results

4. GUARDIAN ACTIONS
- Playbook executions
- Individual step results
- Rollback events

IMPLEMENTATION:
pub struct AuditEvent {
    pub ts: DateTime<Utc>,
    pub event_type: AuditEventType,
    pub actor: String,
    pub machine_id: Option<String>,
    pub action: String,
    pub result: AuditResult,
    pub details: serde_json::Value,
}

pub trait Auditable {
    fn to_audit_event(&self) -> AuditEvent;
}

// Implement for all actionable types
impl Auditable for CollectorRun { ... }
impl Auditable for AutopilotAction { ... }
impl Auditable for GuardianStep { ... }

CLI COMMANDS:
vc audit list                        # Recent audit events
vc audit list --type guardian        # Filter by type
vc audit list --machine orko         # Filter by machine
vc audit list --since 24h            # Time filter
vc audit show <event_id>             # Full details

RETENTION:
- Keep detailed audit for 90 days
- Aggregate to daily summaries forever

TESTS:
- Test audit event creation for each type
- Test filtering and searching
- Test retention/aggregation

## Testing & Logging
- Unit tests for audit event normalization, enum mapping, and JSON serialization.
- Integration tests that verify collector runs, alert triggers, and guardian actions emit audit_events rows with correct details.
- E2E: `tests/e2e/system/test_audit_trail.sh` exercising poll → alert → guardian → cli actions; assert row counts and field integrity.
- Structured logs and JSON summary in `tests/logs/` including event counts by type and failure reasons.

title: Implement ntm (Named Tmux Manager) collector
description:
Implement collector for ntm (Named Tmux Manager) - the nerve center of agent orchestration.

INTEGRATION METHOD:
- Shell out to ntm --robot-status and ntm --robot-list for JSON output
- Optionally parse events.jsonl if ntm provides it
- Use CLI Snapshot pattern (stateless)

DUCKDB TABLES:
CREATE TABLE ntm_sessions_snapshot(
    machine_id TEXT,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    session_name TEXT,
    work_dir TEXT,
    git_branch TEXT,
    agent_counts_json TEXT,
    panes_json TEXT,
    raw_json TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (machine_id, collected_at, session_name)
);

CREATE TABLE ntm_activity_snapshot(
    machine_id TEXT,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    total_agents INTEGER,
    by_type_json TEXT,
    by_state_json TEXT,
    raw_json TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (machine_id, collected_at)
);

CREATE TABLE ntm_events(
    machine_id TEXT,
    ts TIMESTAMP,
    event_type TEXT,
    session_name TEXT,
    agent_id TEXT,
    details_json TEXT
);

COLLECTOR IMPLEMENTATION:
- Implement Collector trait for NtmCollector
- Handle missing ntm binary gracefully (fail-soft)
- Parse JSON output from ntm --robot-status
- Extract session details: name, work_dir, branch, agent counts
- Store raw_json for forward compatibility

UI VALUE:
- Agent fleet map by machine/repo/session
- Real-time view of which agents are working where
- Session health monitoring

TESTS:
- Mock ntm CLI outputs
- Verify table population

title: Implement health score calculation system
description:
Implement the health score calculation system in vc_query crate.

PURPOSE:
Compute health scores for machines and the overall fleet based on multiple factors. This is the core intelligence that powers the at a glance view.

DATA MODEL (from plan):
CREATE TABLE health_factors(
    machine_id TEXT,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    factor_id TEXT,
    severity TEXT,
    score REAL,
    details_json TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE health_summary(
    machine_id TEXT,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    overall_score REAL,
    worst_factor_id TEXT,
    details_json TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (machine_id, collected_at)
);

HEALTH FACTORS TO COMPUTE:
1. System resources (CPU, memory, disk, load)
2. Agent velocity (tokens/min, stuck detection)
3. Rate limit headroom (time to limit)
4. DCG denies (frequency and severity)
5. Network anomalies (unknown providers)
6. RCH queue depth
7. Mail backlog (urgent unread)
8. Repo cleanliness (dirty count)
9. Process issues (zombies, stuck)
10. Data freshness + drift (from collector_health/drift_events)

SCORING ALGORITHM:
- Weighted average with worst-factor penalty
- Each factor has weight and score (0.0=critical to 1.0=healthy)
- Apply penalty for critical factors (each critical = -0.1)
- Penalize stale data (freshness_seconds over threshold reduces score)
- Final score clamped to [0.0, 1.0]

THRESHOLDS (configurable):
- Disk: less than 10% free = critical, less than 20% = warning
- CPU: greater than 90% for 5m = critical, greater than 75% for 5m = warning
- Load: greater than 2x cores = critical, greater than 1.5x cores = warning
- Rate limit: less than 10min to limit = critical, less than 30min = warning
- Agent velocity: less than 10 tok/m for 15m = critical
- Freshness: collector stale beyond threshold reduces score

INTEGRATION:
- Called after each poll cycle
- Results stored in health_factors and health_summary tables
- Used by TUI overview, web dashboard, alerts, autopilot

TESTS:
- Test factor computation for each metric type
- Test weighted scoring algorithm
- Test threshold classification
- Test fleet-wide aggregation

## Testing & Logging
- Unit tests for factor weights, thresholds, penalty rules, and score clamping.
- Fixture-based tests for multi-factor scenarios (disk critical + mail backlog + dcg spikes).
- Integration tests: insert synthetic data into DuckDB and validate health_summary outputs.
- E2E: `tests/e2e/system/test_health_scores.sh` to verify score behavior under controlled inputs.
- Structured logs: factors, weights, penalties, final_score, worst_factor_id.

title: Implement fleet orchestration commands
description:
Implement fleet orchestration commands for advanced agent management.

PURPOSE:
Provide high-level commands for scaling, load balancing, migration, and emergency operations across the agent fleet.

FLEET COMMANDS (from plan Section 6.3):

1. SPAWN AGENTS
vc fleet spawn --type claude --count 5 --machine gpu-box --model opus
- Spawns new agent instances on target machine
- Validates machine capacity before spawning
- Respects rate limits and quotas

2. REBALANCE FLEET
vc fleet rebalance --strategy even-load --preserve-locality
Strategies:
- even-load: Distribute by CPU/memory
- even-agents: Equal agent counts
- cost-optimized: Prefer cheaper machines
- latency-optimized: Keep agents near their repos

3. EMERGENCY STOP
vc fleet emergency-stop --scope machine:orko --reason "maintenance"
Scopes: machine, provider, all, agent-type
- Graceful shutdown with state preservation
- Requires confirmation unless --force
- Logs to audit trail

4. MIGRATE WORKLOAD
vc fleet migrate --from orko --to sydneymc --workload "repo:smartedgar*"
- Moves active workloads between machines
- Supports glob patterns for workload selection
- Tracks migration progress

5. CANARY DEPLOY
vc fleet canary --config new_prompt_v2.toml --percentage 10 --auto-rollback
- Deploy new config to subset of agents
- Monitor for regressions
- Auto-rollback if metrics degrade

DATA MODEL:
CREATE TABLE fleet_commands(
    command_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    command_type TEXT NOT NULL,
    params_json TEXT NOT NULL,
    status TEXT NOT NULL,
    started_at TIMESTAMP,
    completed_at TIMESTAMP,
    result_json TEXT,
    error_message TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE fleet_migrations(
    migration_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    from_machine TEXT,
    to_machine TEXT,
    workload_spec TEXT,
    status TEXT,
    agents_migrated INTEGER,
    agents_total INTEGER,
    started_at TIMESTAMP,
    completed_at TIMESTAMP
);

IMPLEMENTATION:
- vc_guardian crate handles execution
- Integrate with ntm for agent spawning/stopping
- Validate machine capabilities before operations
- Full audit logging

TESTS:
- Test dry-run mode for all commands
- Test emergency stop with mock agents
- Test rebalance strategy calculations
- Test canary rollback triggers

## Testing & Logging

- Integration tests with SSH runner mocks and audit trail verification.
- E2E: `tests/e2e/system/test_fleet_commands.sh` using localhost/mocks; verify no destructive actions without explicit flags; JSON summary + audit events.

title: Migrate vibe_cockpit from Tokio to Asupersync
notes:
We must completely eradicate Tokio from the vibe_cockpit ecosystem, migrating exclusively to the /dp/asupersync project. This represents a fundamental architectural shift from ad-hoc async/await to rigorous, region-bound structured concurrency leveraging 'Cx' capabilities.

Crucially, this migration must be seamless: DO NOT OVERSIMPLIFY. ALL features, timeouts, retry logic, error handling, and performance characteristics must be preserved or improved. We will handle deeply entrenched third-party crates (like russh, axum) by surgically routing them through the asupersync-tokio-compat bridge if native Asupersync alternatives are not yet mature enough. 

Every phase of this migration must be backed by newly written E2E test scripts, comprehensive unit testing leveraging Asupersync's deterministic test lab, and high-fidelity tracing logs to guarantee the system works perfectly after implementation.

title: Implement retention policy and vc vacuum command
description:
Implement retention policy system and vc vacuum command.

PURPOSE:
Manage data lifecycle to prevent unbounded storage growth while preserving critical history.

RETENTION POLICY (from plan Section 5.3):
| Data Type | Full Resolution | Aggregates |
|-----------|-----------------|------------|
| System samples | 7-30 days | Forever |
| Event logs (dcg, rano) | 90 days raw | Forever |
| Snapshots | 30 days | Forever |
| Derived rollups | N/A | Forever |

DUCKDB SCHEMA:
CREATE TABLE retention_policies(
    policy_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    table_name TEXT NOT NULL,
    retention_days INTEGER NOT NULL,
    aggregate_table TEXT,
    enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    last_vacuum_at TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE retention_log(
    ts TIMESTAMP,
    policy_id TEXT,
    table_name TEXT,
    rows_deleted BIGINT,
    rows_aggregated BIGINT,
    duration_ms BIGINT
);

CLI COMMANDS:
vc vacuum                    # Run all retention policies
vc vacuum --dry-run          # Show what would be deleted
vc vacuum --table sys_samples  # Run for specific table
vc retention list            # Show all policies
vc retention set --table sys_samples --days 14

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Before deleting: aggregate data to rollup tables
2. Use transactions for safety
3. Log all deletions to retention_log
4. Respect no deletion discipline - make retention OPT-IN and transparent
5. Support Parquet export before truncation

AGGREGATION EXAMPLES:
- sys_samples: aggregate to hourly/daily averages
- dcg_events: aggregate to daily counts by severity
- repo_status_snapshots: keep only latest per day

SAFETY:
- Never delete without explicit user configuration
- Show preview before deletion
- Maintain audit trail
- Support recovery via Parquet archives

TESTS:
- Test dry-run mode
- Test aggregation correctness
- Test retention_log entries
- Test Parquet export/archive

## Testing & Logging
- Unit tests for policy parsing, retention math, and dry-run output formatting.
- Integration tests with temporary DuckDB: aggregate-then-delete workflow, rollback on failure.
- E2E: `tests/e2e/system/test_retention_vacuum.sh` verifying opt-in behavior, log entries, and Parquet export; JSON summary in tests/logs/.

title: Phase 0: Foundation - Core Infrastructure
description:
# Phase 0: Foundation - Core Infrastructure

## Purpose
Establish the foundational infrastructure for vibe_cockpit: the Cargo workspace, configuration system, database layer, and basic CLI. This phase creates the scaffolding upon which all other features are built.

## Key Deliverables
1. **Cargo Workspace**: Multi-crate workspace with proper organization
2. **Config System**: TOML-based configuration with validation
3. **DuckDB Layer**: Schema migrations, connection pooling, query helpers
4. **Collector Trait**: The standardized interface for all data collectors
5. **Basic CLI**: clap-based CLI with `vc robot health` stub

## Success Criteria
- `cargo build --workspace` succeeds
- `vc --version` shows build info
- `vc robot health --json` returns valid JSON envelope
- DuckDB file created with migration tracking
- Config file parsing works with sensible defaults

## Technical Context

### Why Cargo Workspace?
The 12-crate architecture (vc_config, vc_collect, vc_store, etc.) keeps concerns separated:
- Faster incremental builds
- Clear dependency boundaries
- Easier testing of individual components
- Future-proofed for selective compilation

### Why DuckDB?
DuckDB is the analytics backbone because:
- Embedded (no server process)
- Columnar storage ideal for time-series analytics
- SQL interface familiar to operators
- Easy export to Parquet/JSON for external tools
- OLAP optimized for the read-heavy query patterns we need

### Why Pull-First Architecture?
Starting with pull-only SSH-based collection:
- Zero deployment footprint on remote machines
- Simpler security model (only SSH access needed)
- Easier debugging (all logic centralized)
- Push can be added later for scale

## Dependencies
None - this is the foundation that everything else depends on.

## Estimated Scope
- ~1-2 weeks of focused work
- Creates the patterns that all subsequent phases follow

## Testing & E2E Expectations
- Every deliverable in this phase must add unit tests per bd-1x9.
- Extend bd-30z with phase-specific e2e scenarios and structured logs.
- Phase completion requires `cargo test --workspace` + phase e2e suite with JSON summaries in tests/logs/.

title: Create Cargo workspace with 12-crate architecture
description:
# Task: Create Cargo workspace with 12-crate architecture

## What to Build
Initialize a Cargo workspace in /data/projects/vibe_cockpit with 12 internal crates following the architecture spec.

## Crate Structure
```
vibe_cockpit/
├── Cargo.toml (workspace)
├── vc.toml (runtime config, created later)
├── crates/
│   ├── vc_config/       # Config parsing + validation, secrets/paths
│   ├── vc_collect/      # Collectors (one per upstream tool + system collectors)
│   ├── vc_store/        # DuckDB schema migrations + ingestion helpers + query library
│   ├── vc_query/        # Canonical queries (health, rollups, leaderboards, anomalies)
│   ├── vc_oracle/       # Prediction engine, pattern recognition, forecasting
│   ├── vc_guardian/     # Self-healing protocols, fleet orchestration
│   ├── vc_knowledge/    # Solution mining, gotcha database, playbooks
│   ├── vc_alert/        # Rule engine + notifications + autopilot hooks
│   ├── vc_tui/          # ratatui UI + navigation + charts
│   ├── vc_web/          # axum server + embedded static web assets + JSON API
│   ├── vc_cli/          # clap commands; robot mode output formatting
│   └── vc_mcp/          # MCP server mode for agent queries
└── src/
    └── main.rs          # Binary entry point
```

## Workspace Cargo.toml Template
```toml
[workspace]
resolver = "2"
members = [
    "crates/vc_config",
    "crates/vc_collect",
    "crates/vc_store",
    "crates/vc_query",
    "crates/vc_oracle",
    "crates/vc_guardian",
    "crates/vc_knowledge",
    "crates/vc_alert",
    "crates/vc_tui",
    "crates/vc_web",
    "crates/vc_cli",
    "crates/vc_mcp",
]

[workspace.package]
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"
authors = ["vibe_cockpit contributors"]
license = "MIT"
repository = "https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/vibe_cockpit"

[workspace.dependencies]
# Async runtime
tokio = { version = "1.44", features = ["full"] }

# Database
duckdb = { version = "1.1", features = ["bundled"] }

# Serialization
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1.0"
toml = "0.8"

# CLI
clap = { version = "4.5", features = ["derive"] }

# TUI
ratatui = "0.29"
crossterm = "0.28"

# Web
axum = "0.7"
tower = "0.5"
tower-http = { version = "0.6", features = ["cors", "fs", "trace"] }

# SSH (for remote execution)
# russh = "0.45"  # Consider later; MVP uses ssh binary

# Time
chrono = { version = "0.4", features = ["serde"] }

# Error handling
thiserror = "2.0"
anyhow = "1.0"

# Tracing
tracing = "0.1"
tracing-subscriber = { version = "0.3", features = ["env-filter", "json"] }

# Other utilities
uuid = { version = "1.11", features = ["v4", "serde"] }
regex = "1.11"
```

## Each Crate's Initial Cargo.toml
Each crate should:
- Use `[package]` with `version.workspace = true`, `edition.workspace = true`
- Declare minimal dependencies from workspace
- Export a `lib.rs` with placeholder types

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace` succeeds
- [ ] Each crate compiles independently
- [ ] No circular dependencies between crates
- [ ] Binary produces help output: `cargo run -- --help`

## Technical Rationale
The 12-crate split keeps concerns separated:
- **vc_config**: Pure data structures, no I/O (enables testing)
- **vc_collect**: Plugin-like collectors, each can evolve independently
- **vc_store**: Single point of DuckDB access (connection pooling, migrations)
- **vc_query**: Reusable query library (shared by TUI, web, CLI)
- **vc_oracle/guardian/knowledge**: Intelligence layers can be developed in parallel
- **vc_alert**: Separate from oracle to allow simpler rule-based alerts
- **vc_tui/web/cli**: Three UI surfaces share vc_query but have different dependencies
- **vc_mcp**: Optional feature, can be compiled out if not needed

## Notes for Future Self
- Start with stub implementations that compile
- Don't over-engineer initial versions - just get the structure right
- Cross-crate dependencies will be refined as features are built

## E2E & Logging
- Add scenario scripts under `tests/e2e/<area>/` with structured logs and JSON summaries.

    error_message TEXT,

);

IMPLEMENTATION:
pub trait AlertChannel: Send + Sync {
    fn name(&self) -> &str;
    async fn deliver(&self, alert: &Alert) -> Result<(), ChannelError>;
    fn supports_severity(&self, severity: Severity) -> bool;
}

pub struct ChannelManager {
    channels: Vec<Box<dyn AlertChannel>>,

    pub async fn deliver_all(&self, alert: &Alert) -> Vec<DeliveryResult> {
        let futures = self.channels.iter()
            .filter(|c| c.supports_severity(alert.severity))
            .map(|c| c.deliver(alert));
        join_all(futures).await
    }
}

title: Phase 2a: Migrate vc_collect to Asupersync
notes:
Background: vc_collect heavily relies on Tokio's process execution, time/sleep utilities, and synchronization primitives, plus the russh crate for async SSH. We must transition these to Asupersync while meticulously preserving timeout behaviors, polling logic, and error recovery.

Tasks:
1. Replace tokio::process::Command with asupersync::process::Command for local shell executions (e.g. ntm, git, etc.). Ensure stdout/stderr streams are correctly mapped.
2. Replace tokio::time::timeout and tokio::time::sleep with asupersync::time equivalents. 
3. Migrate internal async Mutexes and RwLocks (e.g., within SshRunner) to asupersync::sync equivalents, paying careful attention to deadlock prevention.
4. Handling 'russh': Wrap the russh SSH client using asupersync-tokio-compat. Ensure connection pooling logic remains optimal and explicit cancellation via Cx correctly aborts stalled SSH handshakes.
5. Add rigorous tracing and latency tracking for collection cycles using Asupersync observability tools to ensure the migration doesn't introduce polling delays.

Testing & Validation:
- Unit Tests: Create deterministic tests for polling interval logic, validating that timeouts fire exactly when expected.
- E2E Tests: Write e2e_collect_ssh.sh to spin up a local SSH daemon, point vc_collect at it, simulate network latency, and assert timeouts. Validate collection latency percentiles do not degrade.
- Logging: Ensure high-fidelity tracing over SSH connection states and command execution duration.
close_reason: done

title: Phase 2: Accounts + Sessions + Mail
description:
# Phase 2: Accounts + Sessions + Mail

## Purpose
Integrate account management (caut/caam), session indexing (cass), and inter-agent coordination (mcp_agent_mail). This phase enables the critical rate limit forecasting feature - the Predictive Oracle MVP.

## Key Deliverables
1. **caut Collector**: Per-account usage tracking via `caut usage --json`
2. **caam Collector**: Profile limits and status via `caam limits --format json`, `caam status --json`
3. **cass Collector**: Session statistics and timeline via `cass stats/timeline/health --json`
4. **mcp_agent_mail Collector**: Message/reservation tracking via SQLite incremental queries
5. **TUI Screens**: Accounts, Sessions, Mail views
6. **Rate Limit Forecasting**: Oracle MVP predicting time-to-limit and suggesting swaps

## Success Criteria
- Account usage visible across all providers (Claude, OpenAI, Gemini)
- Rate limit forecasts accurate within 10 minutes
- Mail backlog surfaced with urgency indicators
- Session volume trends visible

## Technical Context

### Account System Architecture
caut tracks usage windows, caam manages profiles:
- caut: "How much have I used?" (usage percentage, reset times)
- caam: "Which account should I use?" (profile selection, health scores)

Together they enable intelligent account rotation before hitting limits.

### mcp_agent_mail SQLite Integration
The SQLite incremental pattern:
- Keep cursor of last-seen message_id
- Query `WHERE id > cursor ORDER BY id LIMIT N`
- Update cursor on success

This avoids rescanning the entire message history each poll.

### Oracle MVP - Rate Limit Forecasting
The first prediction capability:
```rust
pub struct RateLimitForecast {
    pub time_to_limit: Duration,
    pub confidence: f64,
    pub recommended_action: RateLimitAction, // Continue, SlowDown, PrepareSwap, SwapNow
    pub optimal_swap_time: Option<Instant>,
    pub alternative_accounts: Vec<(AccountId, f64)>,
}
```

Calculated from: current_usage_pct + velocity (consumption rate) + historical patterns.

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 1 (basic collectors working, TUI framework in place)

## Estimated Scope
- ~2 weeks
- Enables proactive rate limit management - high user value

## Testing & E2E Expectations
- Every deliverable in this phase must add unit tests per bd-1x9.
- Extend bd-30z with phase-specific e2e scenarios and structured logs.
- Phase completion requires `cargo test --workspace` + phase e2e suite with JSON summaries in tests/logs/.

title: Implement caut (usage tracker) collector
description:
# Task: Implement caut (usage tracker) collector

## What to Build
A collector for per-account usage tracking via the caut tool. Uses CLI Snapshot pattern.

## Integration Method
```bash
caut usage --json
```

## Expected JSON Schema
```json
{
  "accounts": [
    {
      "provider": "claude",
      "account": "jeff@email.com",
      "window": "5_hour",
      "used_percent": 45.0,
      "remaining_percent": 55.0,
      "resets_at": "2026-01-27T05:00:00Z",
      "credits_remaining": null,
      "status": "healthy"
    },
    {
      "provider": "openai",
      "account": "dev@company.com",
      "window": "daily",
      "used_percent": 78.0,
      "remaining_percent": 22.0,
      "resets_at": "2026-01-28T00:00:00Z",
      "credits_remaining": 85.50,
      "status": "warning"
    }
  ]
}
```

## Collector Implementation
```rust
// crates/vc_collect/src/collectors/caut.rs

pub struct CautCollector;

#[async_trait]
impl Collector for CautCollector {
    fn name(&self) -> &'static str { "caut" }
    fn schema_version(&self) -> u32 { 1 }
    fn required_tool(&self) -> Option<&'static str> { Some("caut") }
    
    async fn collect(&self, ctx: &CollectContext) -> CollectResult {
        let output = ctx.executor.run_timeout("caut usage --json", ctx.timeout).await?;
        let data: CautUsageOutput = serde_json::from_str(&output)?;
        let collected_at = Utc::now();
        
        let rows: Vec<_> = data.accounts.iter().map(|a| {
            serde_json::json!({
                "machine_id": ctx.machine_id.as_str(),
                "collected_at": collected_at.to_rfc3339(),
                "provider": a.provider,
                "account": a.account,
                "window": a.window,
                "used_percent": a.used_percent,
                "resets_at": a.resets_at,
                "credits_remaining": a.credits_remaining,
                "status": a.status,
                "raw_json": serde_json::to_string(a).unwrap_or_default(),
            })
        }).collect();
        
        CollectResult {
            rows: vec![RowBatch { table: "account_usage_snapshots", rows }],
            new_cursor: None,
            ..Default::default()
        }
    }
}
```

## DuckDB Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS account_usage_snapshots(
    machine_id TEXT,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    provider TEXT,
    account TEXT,
    window TEXT,
    used_percent REAL,
    resets_at TIMESTAMP,
    credits_remaining REAL,
    status TEXT,
    raw_json TEXT
);

CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_account_usage_time 
ON account_usage_snapshots(machine_id, provider, account, collected_at DESC);
```

## Key Use Cases
1. **Rate limit monitoring**: Show usage % approaching limits
2. **Reset time tracking**: Know when accounts refresh
3. **Cross-account comparison**: Which account has most headroom?
4. **Historical trends**: Usage patterns over time

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Collector parses caut JSON correctly
- [ ] account_usage_snapshots table populated
- [ ] All providers tracked (claude, openai, gemini)
- [ ] Graceful handling when caut unavailable

## Notes for Future Self
- caut has both token-accounts.json (static) and usage-history.sqlite (historical)
- Consider reading SQLite directly for historical charts
- This feeds directly into Oracle rate limit forecasting

## Testing & Logging
Unit tests:

- Validate normalized fields and schema_version handling.
- Error-path tests: missing tool, non-zero exit, timeout, invalid JSON/JSONL.

Integration tests:

- Emit structured logs: collector, machine_id, duration_ms, rows_inserted, stderr.
- Write JSON summary artifact to `tests/logs/`.

title: Implement mcp_agent_mail SQLite collector
description:
# Task: Implement mcp_agent_mail SQLite collector

## What to Build
A collector that reads from mcp_agent_mail's SQLite database using the incremental primary key pattern.

## Integration Method
Direct SQLite query on `storage.sqlite3`:
```
~/.mcp_agent_mail_git_mailbox_repo/storage.sqlite3
```

## SQLite Incremental Pattern
This collector uses the "SQLite Incremental" pattern:
1. Keep cursor of last-seen message_id
2. Query `WHERE id > cursor ORDER BY id LIMIT N`
3. Update cursor on success

```rust
pub struct AgentMailCollector {
    db_path: PathBuf,
}

#[async_trait]
impl Collector for AgentMailCollector {
    fn name(&self) -> &'static str { "mcp_agent_mail" }
    fn schema_version(&self) -> u32 { 1 }
    
    async fn collect(&self, ctx: &CollectContext) -> CollectResult {
        let last_id = match &ctx.last_cursor {
            Some(Cursor::PrimaryKey(id)) => *id,
            _ => 0,
        };
        
        let collected_at = Utc::now();
        let mut rows = vec![];
        let mut max_id = last_id;
        
        // Query messages
        let query = format!(
            r#"
            SELECT id, project_id, thread_id, sender, 
                   importance, ack_required, created_ts, subject
            FROM messages 
            WHERE id > {} 
            ORDER BY id 
            LIMIT {}
            "#,
            last_id, ctx.max_rows
        );
        
        let messages = ctx.executor.sqlite_query(&self.db_path, &query).await?;
        
        for msg in &messages {
            let id: i64 = msg["id"].as_i64().unwrap_or(0);
            max_id = max_id.max(id);
            
            rows.push(serde_json::json!({
                "collected_at": collected_at.to_rfc3339(),
                "project_id": msg["project_id"],
                "message_id": id,
                "thread_id": msg["thread_id"],
                "sender": msg["sender"],
                "importance": msg["importance"],
                "ack_required": msg["ack_required"],
                "created_ts": msg["created_ts"],
                "subject": msg["subject"],
                "raw_json": serde_json::to_string(msg).unwrap_or_default(),
            }));
        }
        
        // Query file reservations (snapshot, not incremental)
        let reservation_query = r#"
            SELECT id, project_id, path_pattern, holder, expires_ts, exclusive
            FROM file_reservations
            WHERE expires_ts > datetime('now')
        "#;
        
        let reservations = ctx.executor.sqlite_query(&self.db_path, reservation_query).await?;
        
        let reservation_rows: Vec<_> = reservations.iter().map(|r| {
            serde_json::json!({
                "collected_at": collected_at.to_rfc3339(),
                "project_id": r["project_id"],
                "reservation_id": r["id"],
                "path_pattern": r["path_pattern"],
                "holder": r["holder"],
                "expires_ts": r["expires_ts"],
                "exclusive": r["exclusive"],
                "raw_json": serde_json::to_string(r).unwrap_or_default(),
            })
        }).collect();
        
        CollectResult {
            rows: vec![
                RowBatch { table: "mail_messages", rows },
                RowBatch { table: "mail_file_reservations", rows: reservation_rows },
            ],
            new_cursor: Some(Cursor::PrimaryKey(max_id)),
            ..Default::default()
        }
    }
}
```

## DuckDB Tables
```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS mail_messages(
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    project_id TEXT,
    message_id INTEGER,
    thread_id TEXT,
    sender TEXT,
    importance TEXT,
    ack_required BOOLEAN,
    created_ts TIMESTAMP,
    subject TEXT,
    raw_json TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (project_id, message_id)
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS mail_recipients(
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    message_id INTEGER,
    recipient TEXT,
    read_ts TIMESTAMP,
    ack_ts TIMESTAMP,
    raw_json TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS mail_file_reservations(
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    project_id TEXT,
    reservation_id INTEGER,
    path_pattern TEXT,
    holder TEXT,
    expires_ts TIMESTAMP,
    exclusive BOOLEAN,
    raw_json TEXT
);
```

## Key Queries
```sql
-- Urgent unread messages
SELECT * FROM mail_messages
WHERE importance IN ('high', 'urgent')
  AND ack_required = true
  AND message_id NOT IN (
    SELECT message_id FROM mail_recipients WHERE ack_ts IS NOT NULL
  );

-- Active file reservations
SELECT * FROM mail_file_reservations
WHERE expires_ts > CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;

-- Message volume by thread
SELECT thread_id, COUNT(*) as msg_count
FROM mail_messages
GROUP BY thread_id
ORDER BY msg_count DESC;
```

## Alert Rules
- Mail backlog: > 5 urgent unread
- Mail overdue: > 3 ack_required unacked > 1 hour

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Incremental message collection works
- [ ] Cursor persisted across poll cycles
- [ ] File reservations captured
- [ ] Handles missing SQLite file gracefully

## Notes for Future Self
- SQLite path may vary per machine - make configurable
- Consider recipients table for read/ack tracking
- This is critical for agent coordination visibility

## Testing & Logging
Unit tests:
- Parse representative fixtures (happy path + partial/malformed).
- Validate normalized fields and schema_version handling.
- Error-path tests: missing tool, non-zero exit, timeout, invalid JSON/JSONL.

Integration tests:
- Insert rows into DuckDB and query back expected values (including raw_json/raw_output).
- Cursor/incremental window idempotency (no duplicates on re-run).

E2E:
- Add/extend `tests/e2e/collectors/test_<collector>.sh` (see bd-30z).
- Emit structured logs: collector, machine_id, duration_ms, rows_inserted, stderr.
- Write JSON summary artifact to `tests/logs/`.

title: Enable MVCC concurrent writers for multi-collector inserts
description:
Leverage FrankenSQLite MVCC concurrent writer capability for parallel collector data ingestion.

## Why This Matters
Current DuckDB approach: single connection with Arc<Mutex<Connection>> serializes all writes.
FrankenSQLite MVCC: multiple connections can write simultaneously to different pages.
With 14 collectors potentially writing concurrently, MVCC eliminates write contention.

## What To Do
1. Set PRAGMA fsqlite.concurrent_mode = ON during VcStore::open()
2. Set PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL (required for concurrent access)
3. Implement connection pooling: instead of single Arc<Mutex<Connection>>,
   create a pool of N connections (one per collector)
4. Implement retry logic for BUSY_SNAPSHOT conflicts:
   - Begin transaction with BEGIN CONCURRENT
   - Execute writes
   - On COMMIT, if BUSY_SNAPSHOT error: rollback, re-read, retry
   - Max retries: 5 with exponential backoff (10ms, 20ms, 40ms, 80ms, 160ms)
   - Log each retry with tracing::warn!
5. Add conflict observability: periodically query PRAGMA fsqlite.conflict_stats
6. Log conflict rates to tracing spans

## Connection Pool Design
Instead of single Arc<Mutex<Connection>>:
- VcStore holds a pool of connections (e.g., crossbeam ArrayQueue or simple Vec+Mutex)
- acquire_connection() -> ConnectionGuard (returns to pool on Drop)
- Pool size = min(num_collectors, MAX_CONCURRENT_WRITERS) (default: 8)
- Read-only queries can share a single reader connection (WAL allows concurrent reads)
- Write connections use BEGIN CONCURRENT for MVCC

## Retry Pattern
fn execute_with_retry(conn, sql, params, max_retries) -> Result:
  for attempt in 0..max_retries:
    conn.execute("BEGIN CONCURRENT")?
    match conn.execute(sql, params):
      Ok(_) -> match conn.execute("COMMIT"):
        Ok(_) -> return Ok(())
        Err(BusySnapshot) -> conn.execute("ROLLBACK")?, backoff(attempt)
      Err(e) -> conn.execute("ROLLBACK")?, return Err(e)
  return Err(MaxRetriesExceeded)

## Transaction Advisor
Enable PRAGMA fsqlite.txn_advisor to detect:
- Long transactions (long_txn): warn if transaction held > 100ms
- Large read sets (large_read_set): warn if read set > 1000 pages
- Rollback pressure: alert if conflict rate > 10%

- Expose conflict_stats via VcStore API for TUI display
- Log per-collector write latency distribution

title: Implement incident management system
description:
Implement incident tracking and management for correlating events and enabling post-incident review.

INCIDENT MODEL:
An incident is a bounded time range with a root cause hypothesis and a reconstructed timeline. Used for post-mortem analysis and pattern learning.

DUCKDB TABLES (already defined in schema):
- incidents: Core incident record with status, severity, title
- incident_timeline_events: Correlated events from multiple sources
- incident_notes: Human/agent annotations during incident

INCIDENT LIFECYCLE:
1. AUTO-DETECTED: Created when multiple alerts correlate
2. OPEN: Active investigation
3. MITIGATED: Immediate issue resolved
4. CLOSED: Root cause identified, postmortem complete

INCIDENT CREATION:
- Auto-create when multiple related alerts fire within time window
- Manual creation via TUI or CLI
- Guardian can create incidents during healing failures

CLI COMMANDS:
vc incident list                    # List recent incidents
vc incident show <id>               # Show incident details + timeline
vc incident create --title "..."   # Create manual incident
vc incident note <id> "..."        # Add note to incident
vc incident close <id> --reason "..."  # Close with resolution
vc incident timeline <id>           # Show correlated events

TIMELINE RECONSTRUCTION:
- Collect all events within incident time window
- Correlate across sources: alerts, dcg, rano, pt, collectors
- Order by timestamp
- Identify probable root cause
- Store in incident_timeline_events

INCIDENT AUTO-DETECTION RULES:
- 3+ alerts on same machine within 5 minutes
- Guardian healing failure
- Rate limit hit across multiple accounts
- System crash or OOM detected

TUI INTEGRATION:
- Add incident indicator to alert view
- Link alerts to their incidents
- Quick-create incident from alert context

KNOWLEDGE INTEGRATION:
- Failed incidents inform gotcha database
- Successful resolutions inform playbooks
- Pattern matching against past incidents

TESTS:
- Incident creation tests
- Timeline correlation tests
- Auto-detection rule tests
- Lifecycle transition tests

## E2E & Logging
- Add `tests/e2e/system/test_incident_flow.sh`: inject alerts, auto-create incident, add notes, close, verify timeline.
- Capture DuckDB snapshots and JSON summaries in tests/logs/.

title: Phase 4: rch + rano + dcg + pt + Alerts + Guardian
description:
# Phase 4: rch + rano + dcg + pt + Alerts + Guardian MVP

## Purpose
Complete the collector suite with specialized tools (rch, rano, dcg, pt) and implement the alert and self-healing systems. This phase adds the reactive and proactive response capabilities.

## Key Deliverables
1. **rch Collector**: Remote compilation status via JSON or Prometheus metrics scrape
2. **rano Collector**: Network activity via `rano export --format jsonl --since <duration>`
3. **dcg Collector**: Blocked commands via JSONL tail of history file
4. **pt Collector**: Process triage findings via `pt robot plan --format json`
5. **Alerts MVP**: Threshold-based rules with persistence and state management
6. **Guardian MVP**: Self-healing protocols for common issues

## Success Criteria
- All 15 source systems have working collectors
- Alert rules fire correctly on thresholds
- Guardian protocols can suggest (and optionally execute) remediation
- Audit log captures all automated actions

## Technical Context

### rch Integration Options
Two approaches based on rch deployment:
1. Prometheus scrape: `curl localhost:9100/metrics` then parse metric lines
2. JSON status: `rch status --json` if available

Prometheus is preferred for dashboards (continuous samples), JSON for simpler polling.

### dcg JSONL Tail Pattern
dcg writes to `~/.config/dcg/history.jsonl`:
- Maintain cursor: (inode, offset)
- On rotation (inode changed): reset to 0 or scan last N minutes
- Parse new lines as dcg events

This is the "JSONL Tail" ingestion pattern from the collector architecture.

### Alert Rules MVP
Concrete thresholds from the plan:

**System:**
| Rule | Threshold |
|------|-----------|
| Disk critical | < 10% free |
| Load high | load5 > cores × 1.5 for > 10 min |
| Swap pressure | > 0 and rising |

**Tooling:**
| Rule | Threshold |
|------|-----------|
| DCG denies spike | > 10/hour or new critical rules |
| Mail backlog | > 5 urgent unread |
| Mail overdue | > 3 ack_required unacked > 1 hour |
| RCH queue saturated | queue > 10, workers = 0 |

### Guardian MVP - Healing Protocols
Each protocol follows the structured pattern:
```rust
HealingProtocol {
    name: "rate_limit_recovery",
    trigger: |state| state.usage_pct > 85,
    diagnosis: vec![check_velocity, forecast_limit, find_alternatives],
    treatment: vec![throttle, prepare_swap, execute_swap_if_urgent],
    verification: confirm_new_account_healthy,
    rollback: Some(revert_to_previous),
    escalation: after_3_failures(alert_human),
}
```

MVP protocols:
1. Rate limit recovery
2. Stuck agent recovery
3. Disk space recovery
4. High load mitigation

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 3 (remote execution working across fleet)

## Estimated Scope
- ~2-3 weeks
- Major milestone: vc becomes autonomous (with guardrails)

## Testing & E2E Expectations
- Every deliverable in this phase must add unit tests per bd-1x9.
- Extend bd-30z with phase-specific e2e scenarios and structured logs.
- Phase completion requires `cargo test --workspace` + phase e2e suite with JSON summaries in tests/logs/.

title: Implement rch (remote compilation helper) collector
description:
## Overview
Implement collector for rch (Remote Compilation Helper) - tracks Rust compilation offloading to powerful remote machines.

## Background
rch offloads cargo builds to Mac Minis or cloud VMs. Tracking compilation metrics helps identify slow crates, cache efficiency, and optimal worker allocation.

## Data Model (DuckDB Schema)
```sql
CREATE TABLE rch_compilations (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    machine_id VARCHAR NOT NULL,          -- Which vc machine ran this
    worker_host VARCHAR,                  -- Which rch worker compiled
    crate_name VARCHAR NOT NULL,
    crate_version VARCHAR,
    profile VARCHAR,                      -- debug, release
    target_triple VARCHAR,
    started_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    completed_at TIMESTAMP,
    duration_ms INTEGER,
    cache_hit BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    cache_key VARCHAR,
    exit_code INTEGER,
    error_msg VARCHAR,
    cpu_time_ms INTEGER,
    peak_memory_mb INTEGER,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE rch_workers (
    worker_id VARCHAR PRIMARY KEY,
    hostname VARCHAR,
    status VARCHAR,                       -- idle, busy, offline
    current_job VARCHAR,
    jobs_completed INTEGER,
    total_cpu_time_ms BIGINT,
    last_heartbeat TIMESTAMP,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()
);

-- Indexes for common queries
CREATE INDEX idx_rch_crate ON rch_compilations(crate_name);
CREATE INDEX idx_rch_started ON rch_compilations(started_at);
CREATE INDEX idx_rch_worker ON rch_compilations(worker_host);
```

## Ingestion Pattern: JSONL Tail
rch writes to `~/.rch/compilations.jsonl`:
```json
{"ts":"2025-01-27T10:00:00Z","crate":"serde","version":"1.0.200","profile":"release","duration_ms":12340,"cache_hit":false,"worker":"mini-1"}
```

## Collector Implementation
```rust
pub struct RchCollector {
    jsonl_path: PathBuf,
}

impl Collector for RchCollector {
    const NAME: &str = "rch";
    const TOOL_NAME: &str = "rch";
    type Cursor = FileOffset;
    
    async fn collect(&self, ctx: &CollectContext) -> Result<CollectResult> {
        let offset = ctx.cursor.unwrap_or(0);
        
        let file = File::open(&self.jsonl_path)?;
        file.seek(SeekFrom::Start(offset))?;
        
        let mut records = Vec::new();
        let mut new_offset = offset;
        
        for line in BufReader::new(file).lines() {
            let line = line?;
            new_offset += line.len() as u64 + 1;  // +1 for newline
            
            let record: RchCompilation = serde_json::from_str(&line)?;
            records.push(record);
        }
        
        Ok(CollectResult {
            table: "rch_compilations",
            records: records.into_iter().map(|r| r.to_row()).collect(),
            new_cursor: Some(Cursor::FileOffset(new_offset)),
            machine_id: ctx.machine_id.clone(),
        })
    }
}

#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct RchCompilation {
    ts: DateTime<Utc>,
    crate_name: String,
    version: Option<String>,
    profile: String,
    duration_ms: u64,
    cache_hit: bool,
    worker: String,
    // ... more fields
}
```

## Key Metrics & Queries
```sql
-- Average compile time by crate (last 24h)
SELECT crate_name, 
       AVG(duration_ms) as avg_ms,
       COUNT(*) as compiles,
       SUM(CASE WHEN cache_hit THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)::FLOAT / COUNT(*) as cache_rate
FROM rch_compilations
WHERE started_at > now() - INTERVAL 1 DAY
GROUP BY crate_name
ORDER BY avg_ms DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Worker utilization
SELECT worker_host,
       COUNT(*) as jobs,
       SUM(duration_ms) / 1000.0 as total_seconds,
       AVG(duration_ms) as avg_ms
FROM rch_compilations
WHERE started_at > now() - INTERVAL 1 HOUR
GROUP BY worker_host;

-- Cache hit rate over time
SELECT date_trunc("hour", started_at) as hour,
       SUM(CASE WHEN cache_hit THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)::FLOAT / COUNT(*) as cache_rate
FROM rch_compilations
WHERE started_at > now() - INTERVAL 7 DAY
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] JSONL tail collector reads rch compilation logs
- [ ] Compilations stored with machine_id context
- [ ] Worker status table maintained
- [ ] Cache hit rate metrics available
- [ ] Per-crate build time tracking works
- [ ] Works on local and remote machines

## Testing & Logging
Unit tests:
- Parse representative fixtures (happy path + partial/malformed).
- Validate normalized fields and schema_version handling.
- Error-path tests: missing tool, non-zero exit, timeout, invalid JSON/JSONL.

Integration tests:
- Insert rows into DuckDB and query back expected values (including raw_json/raw_output).
- Cursor/incremental window idempotency (no duplicates on re-run).

E2E:
- Add/extend `tests/e2e/collectors/test_<collector>.sh` (see bd-30z).
- Emit structured logs: collector, machine_id, duration_ms, rows_inserted, stderr.
- Write JSON summary artifact to `tests/logs/`.

## Remote Compatibility
- Collector must run locally and via SSH runner once bd-3nb.4 exists.
- Route execution through the CollectContext executor abstraction (local or remote).
- Add a remote-path test (localhost or mock) and hook into `tests/e2e/remote/test_remote_collectors.sh` (bd-30z).

title: Implement rano (network observer) collector
description:
## Overview
Implement collector for rano - network activity observer for coding agents. This captures connections, domains, processes, and bandwidth so vc can surface anomalous traffic, auth loops, and provider-level issues.

## Integration Methods
- Primary: `rano export --format jsonl --since <duration>`
- Optional fallback: read `observer.sqlite` directly if export is unavailable
- Incremental cursor: track last event timestamp (and event_id if present) to avoid re-ingest

## DuckDB Tables
```sql
CREATE TABLE net_events (
    machine_id TEXT,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    event_ts TIMESTAMP,
    provider TEXT,
    process_name TEXT,
    pid INTEGER,
    direction TEXT,          -- inbound | outbound
    protocol TEXT,           -- tcp | udp | https | ...
    remote_host TEXT,
    remote_ip TEXT,
    remote_port INTEGER,
    bytes_sent BIGINT,
    bytes_received BIGINT,
    tags_json TEXT,
    raw_json TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (machine_id, event_ts, process_name, remote_host)
);

CREATE TABLE net_session_summary (
    machine_id TEXT,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    provider TEXT,
    process_name TEXT,
    session_count INTEGER,
    bytes_sent BIGINT,
    bytes_received BIGINT,
    error_count INTEGER,
    top_remote_hosts_json TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (machine_id, collected_at, provider, process_name)
);

CREATE TABLE net_anomalies (
    machine_id TEXT,
    detected_at TIMESTAMP,
    anomaly_type TEXT,       -- unknown_provider | spike | auth_loop | burst
    severity TEXT,           -- info | warning | critical
    description TEXT,
    evidence_json TEXT,
    raw_json TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (machine_id, detected_at, anomaly_type)
);
```

## Collector Implementation
- Run export with a bounded window (default 10m) and parse JSONL line-by-line.
- Normalize provider/domain/process fields for stable aggregation.
- Store raw_json for forward compatibility.
- Fail-soft when rano is missing; mark collector_available=false and continue.

## Alert + UI Value
- Populate Events screen network section.
- Alert rules: unknown provider spike, auth-loop detection, bandwidth anomalies.

## Testing & Logging
Unit tests:
- Parse representative JSONL fixtures (happy path + malformed/partial lines).
- Normalize provider/domain/process fields.
- Error-path tests: missing binary, empty export, invalid JSONL.

Integration tests:
- Insert into DuckDB and query back net_events/net_session_summary.
- Cursor behavior: no duplicates across consecutive runs.

E2E:
- Add/extend `tests/e2e/collectors/test_rano_collect.sh` (see bd-30z).
- Script emits structured logs: collector, machine_id, duration_ms, rows_inserted, stderr.
- JSON summary per run stored in `tests/logs/`.

## Remote Compatibility

- Route execution through the CollectContext executor abstraction (local or remote).
- Add a remote-path test (localhost or mock) and hook into `tests/e2e/remote/test_remote_collectors.sh` (bd-30z).

title: Implement dcg (dangerous command guard) collector
description:
## Overview
Implement collector for dcg (Dangerous Command Guard) - tracks blocked dangerous commands and security events.

## Background
dcg intercepts and blocks dangerous shell commands (rm -rf /, git push --force, etc.). Tracking blocked commands helps understand agent behavior patterns and identify potential issues.

## Data Model (DuckDB Schema)
```sql
CREATE TABLE dcg_events (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    machine_id VARCHAR NOT NULL,
    timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    event_type VARCHAR NOT NULL,          -- blocked, allowed_override, config_change
    command VARCHAR NOT NULL,
    working_dir VARCHAR,
    user VARCHAR,
    shell_pid INTEGER,
    parent_process VARCHAR,               -- claude-code, codex, bash, etc.
    rule_matched VARCHAR,                 -- Which dcg rule triggered
    severity VARCHAR,                     -- low, medium, high, critical
    override_reason VARCHAR,              -- If allowed after prompt
    session_id VARCHAR,                   -- Link to cass session if available
    collected_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE dcg_rules (
    rule_id VARCHAR PRIMARY KEY,
    machine_id VARCHAR NOT NULL,
    pattern VARCHAR NOT NULL,             -- Regex or glob pattern
    action VARCHAR NOT NULL,              -- block, warn, log
    severity VARCHAR,
    description VARCHAR,
    enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    created_at TIMESTAMP,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT now()
);

-- Indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_dcg_ts ON dcg_events(timestamp);
CREATE INDEX idx_dcg_type ON dcg_events(event_type);
CREATE INDEX idx_dcg_severity ON dcg_events(severity);
```

## Ingestion Pattern: SQLite Incremental
dcg stores events in `~/.dcg/events.db`:
```sql
-- dcg native schema
CREATE TABLE events (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    ts TEXT,
    type TEXT,
    cmd TEXT,
    cwd TEXT,
    rule TEXT,
    severity TEXT,
    ...
);
```

## Collector Implementation
```rust
pub struct DcgCollector {
    db_path: PathBuf,
}

impl Collector for DcgCollector {
    const NAME: &str = "dcg";
    const TOOL_NAME: &str = "dcg";
    type Cursor = i64;  // Last event ID
    
    async fn collect(&self, ctx: &CollectContext) -> Result<CollectResult> {
        let conn = rusqlite::Connection::open(&self.db_path)?;
        let last_id = ctx.cursor.unwrap_or(0);
        
        let mut stmt = conn.prepare(
            "SELECT id, ts, type, cmd, cwd, rule, severity, override_reason
             FROM events 
             WHERE id > ?
             ORDER BY id"
        )?;
        
        let events: Vec<DcgEvent> = stmt.query_map([last_id], |row| {
            Ok(DcgEvent {
                id: row.get(0)?,
                timestamp: row.get(1)?,
                event_type: row.get(2)?,
                command: row.get(3)?,
                working_dir: row.get(4)?,
                rule_matched: row.get(5)?,
                severity: row.get(6)?,
                override_reason: row.get(7)?,
            })
        })?.collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>()?;
        
        let new_cursor = events.last().map(|e| e.id).unwrap_or(last_id);
        
        Ok(CollectResult {
            table: "dcg_events",
            records: events.into_iter().map(|e| e.to_row()).collect(),
            new_cursor: Some(Cursor::Integer(new_cursor)),
            machine_id: ctx.machine_id.clone(),
        })
    }
}
```

## Key Metrics & Queries
```sql
-- Blocked commands by severity (last 24h)
SELECT severity, 
       COUNT(*) as count,
       array_agg(DISTINCT rule_matched) as rules
FROM dcg_events
WHERE event_type = "blocked"
  AND timestamp > now() - INTERVAL 1 DAY
GROUP BY severity
ORDER BY 
    CASE severity WHEN "critical" THEN 1 WHEN "high" THEN 2 WHEN "medium" THEN 3 ELSE 4 END;

-- Most frequently blocked commands
SELECT command, 
       COUNT(*) as block_count,
       MIN(timestamp) as first_blocked,
       MAX(timestamp) as last_blocked
FROM dcg_events
WHERE event_type = "blocked"
GROUP BY command
ORDER BY block_count DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Blocks by parent process (agent detection)
SELECT parent_process,
       COUNT(*) as blocks,
       COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) as sessions
FROM dcg_events
WHERE event_type = "blocked"
  AND timestamp > now() - INTERVAL 7 DAY
GROUP BY parent_process
ORDER BY blocks DESC;

-- Override patterns (which dangerous commands were allowed)
SELECT command, override_reason, COUNT(*) as times
FROM dcg_events
WHERE event_type = "allowed_override"
GROUP BY command, override_reason
ORDER BY times DESC;
```

## Guardian Integration
dcg events can trigger Guardian alerts:
```rust
// In vc_guardian
impl GuardianRule for DcgCriticalRule {
    fn check(&self, store: &VcStore) -> Option<Alert> {
        let critical_count = store.query(
            "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dcg_events 
             WHERE severity = \"critical\" 
             AND timestamp > now() - INTERVAL 5 MINUTE"
        )?;
        
        if critical_count > 0 {
            Some(Alert::new(
                AlertLevel::Critical,
                "Critical command blocked by dcg",
                "Check dcg_events for details"
            ))
        } else {
            None
        }
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SQLite incremental collector reads dcg events
- [ ] Events stored with machine_id and session linkage
- [ ] Severity classification preserved
- [ ] Override reasons captured for allowed commands
- [ ] Parent process tracking for agent attribution
- [ ] Works on local and remote machines

## Testing & Logging
Unit tests:
- Parse representative fixtures (happy path + partial/malformed).
- Validate normalized fields and schema_version handling.
- Error-path tests: missing tool, non-zero exit, timeout, invalid JSON/JSONL.

Integration tests:
- Insert rows into DuckDB and query back expected values (including raw_json/raw_output).
- Cursor/incremental window idempotency (no duplicates on re-run).

E2E:
- Add/extend `tests/e2e/collectors/test_<collector>.sh` (see bd-30z).
- Emit structured logs: collector, machine_id, duration_ms, rows_inserted, stderr.
- Write JSON summary artifact to `tests/logs/`.

## Remote Compatibility
- Collector must run locally and via SSH runner once bd-3nb.4 exists.
- Route execution through the CollectContext executor abstraction (local or remote).
- Add a remote-path test (localhost or mock) and hook into `tests/e2e/remote/test_remote_collectors.sh` (bd-30z).

title: Implement comprehensive Phase 4 TUI screens (RCH, Alerts, Guardian, Events)
description:
## Overview
Comprehensive TUI screens for Phase 4 collectors and systems: RCH builds, Alerts dashboard, Guardian self-healing, and Events (dcg/rano/pt). This is the canonical bead for ALL Phase 4 TUI work.

## Background
Phase 4 introduces several interconnected monitoring systems that need dedicated TUI screens:
- **RCH**: Remote compilation monitoring with worker status and cache metrics
- **Alerts**: Real-time alert display with acknowledgment and rule management
- **Guardian**: Self-healing protocol status, pending approvals, and execution history
- **Events**: DCG denies, RANO network anomalies, and PT process findings

These screens share navigation patterns and integrate with the global health overview.

## Screen 1: RCH/Build Screen

title: Implement vc watch streaming mode
description:
Implement vc watch command for real-time JSONL event streaming.

PURPOSE:
Enable guardian agents to monitor without polling full snapshots. Events are emitted on change or at fixed intervals.

COMMAND USAGE:
vc watch --format jsonl --interval 30
vc watch --format jsonl --changes-only
vc watch --format toon --interval 60

EVENT TYPES:
- alert: New or escalated alerts
  {"type":"alert","ts":"...","alert_id":"...","severity":"critical","machine":"orko","message":"..."}

- prediction: Forecast risk crossing threshold
  {"type":"prediction","ts":"...","prediction_type":"rate_limit","machine":"orko","confidence":0.85,"action":"swap_now"}

- opportunity: Optimization hints
  {"type":"opportunity","ts":"...","opportunity_type":"cost_saving","estimated_savings":12.50,"action":"..."}

- health_change: Machine/fleet health state change
  {"type":"health_change","ts":"...","machine":"orko","old_score":0.85,"new_score":0.62,"factor":"cpu_high"}

- collector_status: Collector success/failure
  {"type":"collector_status","ts":"...","collector":"sysmoni","machine":"orko","status":"ok","duration_ms":234}

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Create vc_cli/src/watch.rs
- Use tokio broadcast channel for event distribution
- Support filtering by event type: --events alert,prediction
- Support filtering by machine: --machines orko,sydneymc
- Support severity threshold: --min-severity high

OUTPUT OPTIONS:
- --format jsonl: One JSON object per line (default)
- --format toon: Token-optimized format
- --interval N: Emit summary every N seconds even if no changes
- --changes-only: Only emit when something changes
- --buffer N: Buffer up to N events before emitting (batch mode)

GRACEFUL HANDLING:
- Reconnect to daemon if connection lost
- Emit heartbeat events periodically
- Clean shutdown on SIGINT/SIGTERM

TESTS:
- Event emission tests
- Filter application tests
- Format output tests
- Reconnection tests

## E2E & Logging
- Add `tests/e2e/cli/test_watch_stream.sh` to validate JSONL output, filters, and heartbeat handling.
- Capture stdout/stderr and emit JSON summary in tests/logs/.
close_reason: Watch streaming mode implemented: watch.rs with WatchEvent types (alert, prediction, opportunity, health_change, collector_status, heartbeat), WatchFilter (event types, machines, severity), JSONL/TOON output, expanded CLI args (interval, machines, min-severity, buffer), streaming loop with store polling. 168 tests pass (27 watch-specific).

title: Implement fallback system probe collector
description:
Implement the fallback system probe collector - ALWAYS ENABLED baseline health check that works even when no tools are installed.

INTEGRATION METHOD:
- Raw shell commands that exist on all Linux/macOS systems
- No external tool dependencies
- Always succeeds (or machine is unreachable)

COMMANDS TO RUN:
uptime                      # System uptime and load averages
df -P                       # Disk usage (POSIX format)
free -b                     # Memory usage in bytes
cat /proc/loadavg           # Load averages (Linux)
cat /proc/meminfo           # Detailed memory info (Linux)
cat /proc/stat              # CPU stats (Linux)
sysctl hw.memsize           # Memory (macOS)
vm_stat                     # Memory stats (macOS)

DUCKDB TABLE:
CREATE TABLE sys_fallback_samples(
    machine_id TEXT,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    uptime_seconds BIGINT,
    load1 REAL,
    load5 REAL,
    load15 REAL,
    mem_total_bytes BIGINT,
    mem_available_bytes BIGINT,
    mem_used_bytes BIGINT,
    swap_total_bytes BIGINT,
    swap_used_bytes BIGINT,
    disk_usage_json TEXT,  -- [{mount, total, used, avail, pct}]
    raw_output TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (machine_id, collected_at)
);

COLLECTOR IMPLEMENTATION:
- Implement FallbackProbeCollector with Collector trait
- Detect platform (Linux vs macOS) and use appropriate commands
- Parse uptime output for load averages
- Parse df -P output for disk usage
- Parse free -b or /proc/meminfo for memory
- Store all raw output for debugging
- NEVER FAIL - if individual commands fail, store partial data
- Set collector_available = true always

PRIORITY: This collector runs FIRST and ALWAYS
- Other collectors (sysmoni, etc.) may add more detail
- Fallback ensures we always have basic health data
- Critical for new machines or machines with minimal tooling

UI VALUE:
- Baseline health for ALL machines
- Works immediately on any SSH-accessible system
- Foundation for health scoring when other collectors missing

TESTS:
- Test Linux parsing
- Test macOS parsing
- Test partial failure handling
- Verify always-succeeds behavior

## E2E & Logging
- Add/extend `tests/e2e/collectors/test_<collector>.sh` (see bd-30z).
- Emit structured logs: collector, machine_id, duration_ms, rows_inserted, stderr.
- Write JSON summary artifact to `tests/logs/`.
