title: Implement adaptive polling + on-demand profiling
description:
## Background
Static poll intervals are either too expensive or too slow. We need adaptive scheduling and on-demand high-frequency profiling to react to incidents without permanent overhead.

## Goals
- Adaptive poll scheduler that increases frequency for “hot” machines/collectors.
- On-demand profiling bursts triggered by alerts or manual commands.
- Ensure bounded concurrency and global rate limits still respected.
- Quarantine/backoff for failing collectors to avoid thrash.

## Implementation
### Adaptive Scheduler
- Use collector_health freshness + alert severity to compute per-machine poll intervals.
- Policy: min/max interval (e.g., 15s–5m), exponential backoff on repeated failures.
- Quarantine collectors after N consecutive failures; require manual or timed reset.
- Store decisions in `poll_schedule_decisions` for auditability.
- Configurable per-collector overrides in vc.toml (pin interval, disable adaptive).

### On-Demand Profiling
- New command: `vc profile --machine <id> --duration 120 --interval 2s`.
- Temporarily switch sysmoni to high-frequency mode and capture snapshots.
- Write to `sys_profile_samples` table and link to alert/incident IDs.

### Tables
```sql
CREATE TABLE poll_schedule_decisions(
    machine_id TEXT,
    collector TEXT,
    decided_at TIMESTAMP,
    next_interval_seconds INTEGER,
    reason_json TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE sys_profile_samples(
    machine_id TEXT,
    collected_at TIMESTAMP,
    profile_id TEXT,
    metrics_json TEXT,
    raw_json TEXT
);
```

## Risks
- Over-aggressive polling can overload remote machines.
- Profiling bursts must be rate-limited and opt-in.

## Success Criteria
- Poll intervals adjust based on freshness/alerts without violating max concurrency.
- Failing collectors back off and recover safely.
- Profiling bursts capture high-resolution data linked to incidents.

## Tests & E2E
- Unit tests for interval selection, backoff, and quarantine logic.
- Integration tests for scheduler respecting global limits.
- E2E: `tests/e2e/system/test_adaptive_polling.sh` and `tests/e2e/system/test_profile_burst.sh` with JSON summaries and logs.
close_reason: done

title: Implement incident replay + time‑travel views
description:
## Background
We already track incidents, alerts, and audit events, but there is no “time machine” view to replay what changed and why. Incident replay makes post‑mortems and triage dramatically faster.

## Goals
- Reconstruct fleet state at arbitrary timestamps (time‑travel queries).
- Provide an incident replay view in TUI + web + robot output.
- Link evidence to alerts, audit actions, and collector data.
- Allow export/share of replay snapshots for post‑mortems.

## Implementation
### Core Query Patterns
- Snapshot-at-time: latest snapshot <= T for each table.
- Context window: time range around incident for events (dcg, rano, mail, audit).
- Cache replay snapshots for fast scroll/scrub in UI.

### UI/CLI
- TUI: Incident replay screen with timeline scrubber.
- Web: `/api/incidents/:id/replay?at=...` returning structured JSON.
- CLI: `vc robot incident <id> --at <ts>`.
- Export: `vc incident export <id> --format json|md`.

### Tables
- Reuse `incident_*` tables + `audit_events` and `collector_health`.
- Add `incident_replay_snapshots(incident_id, ts, snapshot_json)` cache for fast replay.

## Risks
- Expensive queries across large tables; use caching and bounded windows.
- Ambiguity in “latest snapshot” if multiple collectors overlap.

## Success Criteria
- Incident replay reproduces health + alerts for known test incidents.
- Replay JSON is stable and documented.
- Exported snapshots can be shared without losing evidence context.

## Tests & E2E
- Unit tests for time‑travel query correctness.
- Regression tests using fixture DuckDB snapshots.
- E2E: `tests/e2e/system/test_incident_replay.sh` with JSON summary and log artifacts.
close_reason: done

title: Implement daily/weekly digest reports
description:
## Background
Operators need a compact daily/weekly digest that summarizes fleet health, alerts, usage, and progress without opening the UI. This is a low‑friction “ops heartbeat.”

## Goals
- Generate Markdown + JSON digests for daily/weekly windows.
- Optionally deliver via MCP Agent Mail or webhook (reuse alert delivery channels where possible).

## Implementation
### Digest Content
- Top alerts + incident counts.
- Health score trends (overall + top 3 machines).
- Usage & rate‑limit forecast summary.
- Beads throughput + top blockers (bv snapshot).
- Notable events (dcg spikes, rano anomalies, pt findings).

### CLI/Automation
- `vc report --window 24h --format md|json`.
- Schedule via cron/systemd or vc daemon tick.

### Tables
- `digest_reports(report_id, window, generated_at, summary_json, markdown)`.

## Risks
- Over‑verbose digests; must stay concise.
- Requires reliable data freshness indicators (bd-new.1).

## Success Criteria
- Digest is <1 page and highlights actionable items.
- Reports are reproducible for the same window.

## Tests & E2E

- Golden‑file tests for Markdown rendering.
- E2E: `tests/e2e/system/test_digest_reports.sh` with JSON summary + logs.

title: Implement vc-node push agent (store‑and‑forward)
description:
## Background
Pull‑only works for small fleets, but as machine count grows it becomes expensive and brittle. A tiny vc‑node enables store‑and‑forward collection when remotes are offline and reduces SSH overhead.

## Goals
- Build a lightweight vc‑node binary that runs locally on remote machines.
- Collect locally, buffer artifacts, and push snapshots to vc‑hub on interval.
- Support offline backfill (store‑and‑forward) with deduplication.

## Implementation
### vc‑node
- Minimal config: hub URL/SSH target, machine_id, enabled collectors.
- Local spool directory for compressed JSONL/Parquet batches.
- Signed manifest per batch (hash + size + schema version).

### Hub Ingest
- Add `vc ingest --from <bundle>` to import snapshots.
- Deduplicate using (machine_id, collector, cursor, payload_hash).
- Record ingest audit events.

### Transfer Modes
- SSH push (scp/rsync) for MVP.
- Optional HTTPS upload later.

## Risks
- Artifact size growth if network is down; require max spool size.
- Duplicate ingestion if dedupe keys are weak.

## Success Criteria
- Remote machine can collect without continuous SSH polling.
- Backfilled bundles ingest cleanly with no duplicates.

## Tests & E2E
- Unit tests for manifest hashing + dedupe keys.
- Integration tests for bundle ingest with synthetic fixtures.
- E2E: `tests/e2e/remote/test_vc_node_push.sh` with JSON summary + logs.
close_reason: done

title: Implement cost attribution analytics
description:

title: Epic: Comprehensive Test Suite & Validation
description:
Build a comprehensive test suite that validates the entire vibe_cockpit system after the three major migrations (FrankenSQLite, FrankenTUI, deep Asupersync). This includes integration tests, E2E scripts, performance benchmarks, and data integrity verification.

## Motivation
The three concurrent migrations (database, TUI, async runtime) touch every crate in the workspace. We need thorough validation that:
- Data flows correctly through the full pipeline (Config → Collect → Store → Query → Display)
- Concurrent writes work correctly with MVCC
- TUI renders all 12 screens without visual regression
- Graceful shutdown completes within bounds
- Performance is acceptable (insert throughput, query latency)
- Existing data can be migrated from DuckDB without loss

## Test Categories
1. **Integration tests** — cross-crate data flow verification
2. **E2E scripts** — startup/shutdown, concurrent writes, TUI rendering
3. **Deterministic tests** — lab runtime scenarios for concurrency bugs
4. **Performance benchmarks** — fsqlite vs duckdb comparison
5. **Data integrity** — migration verification with checksums

## Logging Requirements
All test scripts must include:
- Timestamped step logging (INFO/DEBUG/ERROR)
- Before/after state snapshots for each operation
- Detailed error context on failure (not just "assertion failed")
- Performance timing for each test step
- Summary report at end with pass/fail counts

## Success Criteria
- 100% of existing tests pass after migration
- E2E scripts cover all critical paths
- Lab tests reproduce and verify known concurrency scenarios
- Performance regression < 20% vs DuckDB for typical queries
- Data migration verified with row count and checksum matching

title: Phase 3: Migrate all test suites to Asupersync determinism

2. Replace them with Asupersync's test runner macro or manual asupersync::runtime::Runtime::new().block_on() blocks.
3. Utilize Asupersync's deterministic test labs (src/lab) to rewrite the most critical concurrent tests (e.g., the adaptive scheduler in vc_collect, channel operations in vc_alert), verifying mathematically that no deadlocks or race conditions exist.

Testing & Validation:

title: Translate migrations 010-018 to SQLite-compatible SQL
description:
Port migrations 10-18 from DuckDB syntax to SQLite syntax.

## Migrations to Port (202 lines total, medium complexity)
10. 010_retention_log.sql (20 lines) — Data retention tracking, vacuum logs
11. 011_collector_health.sql (56 lines) — Collector run health/status tracking, 6 indexes
12. 012_health_scores.sql (12 lines) — ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN to health_summary (3 columns)
13. 013_alert_delivery_log.sql (14 lines) — Alert delivery attempt tracking
14. 014_autopilot_decisions.sql (13 lines) — Autonomous remediation decision log
15. 015_incident_notes.sql (15 lines) — Human/agent notes on incidents, 4 indexes (3 on tables from 001)
16. 016_fleet_commands.sql (18 lines) — Fleet-wide command execution tracking, 3 indexes
17. 017_mined_sessions.sql (15 lines) — Sessions mined for knowledge extraction, 2 indexes
18. 018_playbook_autogen.sql (38 lines) — Auto-generated playbook templates, 3 indexes

## Translation Rules (same as bd-dfl)
- TIMESTAMP -> TEXT (ISO8601 strings)
- BOOLEAN -> INTEGER (0/1)
- BIGINT -> INTEGER
- DOUBLE -> REAL
- current_timestamp -> datetime('now')

## Special Considerations
- 012 uses ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN — SQLite supports this (one column per ALTER)
  - If original has multiple ADD COLUMN in one ALTER, split into separate statements
- 015 adds indexes on tables from migration 001 (incidents, incident_timeline)
  - These are cross-migration index additions — valid in SQLite
- 19 CREATE INDEX statements across this batch — all use IF NOT EXISTS
  - SQLite CREATE INDEX syntax is identical, should work as-is
- No array types, no ENUM types, no JSON types in this batch (simpler than 001-009)
- No foreign keys in this batch

## Testing
After translation, verify:
1. Each migration file parses in fsqlite
2. Tables created with correct column types
3. ALTER TABLE statements apply cleanly
4. All 19 indexes created successfully
5. INSERT test data accepted

## Files Changed
- crates/vc_store/src/migrations/010_retention_log.sql through 018_playbook_autogen.sql

title: Implement graceful shutdown with bounded drain
Epic: DuckDB → FrankenSQLite Migration
description:
Complete replacement of DuckDB with FrankenSQLite as the storage backend for Vibe Cockpit.

## Motivation
DuckDB's bundled builds consistently OOM on remote compilation workers (exit 137) and locally (SIGKILL). FrankenSQLite (/dp/frankensqlite) is a ground-up Rust reimplementation of SQLite 3.52.0 that provides:
- **MVCC concurrent writers** (page-level locking, not file-level) — enables parallel collector inserts without contention
- **Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI)** — prevents write skew in concurrent mode
- **Safe Rust engine** (forbid(unsafe_code) in core) — matches our workspace policy
- **Asupersync-native** — no tokio dependency, uses Cx capability tokens throughout
- **MigrationRunner** built-in — replaces our custom migration system
- **RaptorQ self-healing storage** — information-theoretic durability
- **No OOM builds** — pure Rust, no bundled C compilation

## Scope
- Port VcStore from duckdb::Connection to fsqlite::Connection
- Translate all 26 DuckDB migrations to SQLite-compatible SQL
- Port all query methods (handle DuckDB-specific functions like to_json(), LIST types, etc.)
- Port vc_knowledge's direct DuckDB usage
- Enable MVCC concurrent_mode for multi-collector writes with conflict retry
- Update vc_config paths and error types workspace-wide
- Build data migration tool for existing .duckdb files
- Port all unit tests with behavioral parity

## Key Technical Differences
- DuckDB LIST/MAP/STRUCT types → JSON TEXT columns in SQLite
- DuckDB to_json() → manual JSON construction via serde
- DuckDB TIMESTAMP → TEXT (ISO8601) in SQLite
- DuckDB BOOLEAN → INTEGER (0/1) in SQLite
- DuckDB parameterized ? → SQLite ?N or ?
- DuckDB execute_batch() → fsqlite execute() (multi-statement)
- DuckDB analytical functions → SQLite window functions + JSON1
- Memory limit pragma → cache_size pragma
- Thread count pragma → not needed (single-process, MVCC handles concurrency)

## Success Criteria
- Zero DuckDB references in workspace (Cargo.toml, use statements, error types)
- All existing tests pass with FrankenSQLite backend
- Concurrent collector writes verified with MVCC
- Build succeeds on RCH workers without OOM
- Data migration tool handles existing installations

title: Port vc_web raw SQL queries to SQLite-compatible syntax
description:
Port DuckDB-specific SQL in vc_web handlers to SQLite-compatible syntax.

## Scope Assessment
vc_web has 13 store.query_json() calls across its endpoints. After auditing all SQL:
- **12 endpoints use STANDARD SQL** (SELECT * FROM ... WHERE ... LIMIT ... OFFSET ...) — these work as-is once bd-zut ports query_json() to replace to_json() with Rust-side JSON construction.
- **1 endpoint (metrics_handler) has DuckDB-specific SQL** requiring translation.

## metrics_handler DuckDB-Specific SQL (3 patterns)

### 1. EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM ...) — collector freshness calculation (line 567)
DuckDB: EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM current_timestamp) - EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM CAST(collected_at AS TIMESTAMP))
SQLite: (strftime('%s', 'now') - strftime('%s', collected_at))

### 2. COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE ...) — success counting (line 595)
DuckDB: COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE success = true)
SQLite: SUM(CASE WHEN success = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)

### 3. Boolean literal (line 595)
DuckDB: success = true
SQLite: success = 1

## JSON Serialization Verification (ALL 13 endpoints)
After bd-zut replaces to_json() with Rust-side JSON construction, verify:
- **Boolean fields**: SQLite stores 0/1. Rust serde_json must serialize as true/false, not 0/1. Affected: success, is_online, acknowledged, auto_approved, etc.
- **Timestamp fields**: DuckDB TIMESTAMP → SQLite TEXT. Verify ISO 8601 format matches what the Next.js frontend at /web/ parses.
- **NULL handling**: Verify JSON null (not missing key) for nullable columns.
- **Numeric types**: SQLite INTEGER vs REAL — verify f64 columns serialize as floats.

## What To Do

title: Port vc_knowledge from direct DuckDB usage to fsqlite
description:
Port vc_knowledge from direct DuckDB usage to FrankenSQLite API.

## Current Architecture
KnowledgeStore wraps Arc<VcStore> and mixes two data access patterns:
1. **Direct DuckDB**: insert(), get(), add_feedback() use conn.query_row() with duckdb::params![]
2. **VcStore abstraction**: record_view(), record_applied(), delete() use store.execute()
3. **JSON abstraction**: search(), get_by_tags(), recent(), top_rated() use store.query_json()

## DuckDB-Specific Patterns to Replace

### 1. duckdb::params![] macro (lines 329-342, 407-413)
Used in insert() and add_feedback() for parameterized INSERT with RETURNING clause.
Replace with: SqliteValue array parameter binding.

### 2. duckdb::Row type (line 614)
row_to_entry() helper function signature: fn row_to_entry(row: &duckdb::Row<'_>) -> Result<KnowledgeEntry, duckdb::Error>
Replace with: fsqlite Row type and SqliteValue extraction.

### 3. row.get("column_name") pattern (lines 615-650)
13 column extractions using string-based column access.
Replace with: fsqlite row.get() -> SqliteValue match pattern.

### 4. list_contains() DuckDB function (line 530)
Used in get_by_tags() to search TEXT[] array columns:
  WHERE list_contains(tags, $N)
SQLite replacement: WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM json_each(tags) WHERE value = ?)
Note: tags column is TEXT[] in DuckDB, will be JSON TEXT in SQLite.

### 5. ILIKE operator (lines 471-475)
Used in search() for case-insensitive keyword matching:
  WHERE title ILIKE '%pattern%' OR content ILIKE '%pattern%' OR summary ILIKE '%pattern%'
SQLite replacement: WHERE title LIKE '%pattern%' (SQLite LIKE is case-insensitive for ASCII)
Note: If Unicode case-insensitivity needed, use COLLATE NOCASE.

### 6. RETURNING clause (lines 320, 398)
Used in INSERT ... RETURNING id to get auto-generated IDs.
SQLite supports RETURNING since v3.35+ — verify FrankenSQLite support.
Fallback: INSERT then last_insert_rowid().

### 7. Error type: duckdb::Error (line 28)
KnowledgeError::DatabaseError(#[from] duckdb::Error) -> fsqlite::FrankenError

## Methods to Port (by pattern)

### Direct DuckDB (must rewrite):
- insert() — INSERT with RETURNING, duckdb::params![], query_row
- get() — SELECT with query_row, row_to_entry helper
- add_feedback() — INSERT with RETURNING, duckdb::params![]

### VcStore delegation (minimal changes):
- record_view() — uses store.execute()
- record_applied() — uses store.execute()
- recalculate_score() — uses store.execute()
- delete() — uses store.execute()

### JSON abstraction (SQL changes only):
- search() — uses store.query_json(), contains ILIKE
- get_by_tags() — uses store.query_json(), contains list_contains()
- recent() — uses store.query_json(), standard SQL
- top_rated() — uses store.query_json(), standard SQL

### Helper to port:
- row_to_entry() — duckdb::Row -> fsqlite Row, 13 column extractions

## Cargo.toml Change
- Remove: duckdb.workspace = true
- Add: fsqlite.workspace = true (or via vc_store re-export)

## Files Changed
- crates/vc_knowledge/Cargo.toml
- crates/vc_knowledge/src/lib.rs

title: Migrate to Cx-aware asupersync Mutex for async code paths
description:
Replace std::sync::Mutex and tokio::sync::Mutex with asupersync Mutex where used in async context.

## Current Mutex Usage
1. VcStore.conn: Arc<std::sync::Mutex<Connection>>
   -> Used in async context (collectors, web handlers)
   -> Risk: blocking a worker thread while holding lock in async code
   
2. vc_collect SshRunner connections: DashMap (uses internal Mutex)
   -> Used in async context for connection pooling
   -> tokio::sync::Mutex used explicitly

3. vc_alert cooldowns: DashMap<String, Instant>
   -> Concurrent read/write from alert engine

## Why Cx-Aware Mutex
asupersync Mutex::lock(&cx) provides:
- Cancel-safety: lock() returns Err if Cx is cancelled mid-wait
- Observability: lock contention tracked in Cx budget
- Deadlock detection: lab runtime can detect cycles in wait-for graph

## What To Do
1. VcStore.conn: Consider whether to use asupersync Mutex or keep std Mutex
   - If VcStore is used from async code: use asupersync Mutex
   - If VcStore is purely sync (called within spawn_blocking): keep std Mutex
   - With connection pool (see MVCC bead): may not need mutex at all
   
2. SshRunner: Replace tokio::sync::Mutex with asupersync Mutex
   - Update lock() calls to pass &cx
   - Handle Err from cancelled lock

3. Alert cooldowns: DashMap is fine (lock-free concurrent map)
   - No change needed unless we want Cx observability

## Migration Pattern
Before: let guard = mutex.lock().unwrap();
After:  let guard = mutex.lock(&cx).await?;

- crates/vc_store/src/lib.rs
- crates/vc_collect/src/ssh.rs

title: Port VcStore core query/execute methods to fsqlite API
description:
Port all core VcStore methods that execute SQL from DuckDB API to FrankenSQLite API.

## Methods to Port

### execute(sql, params) -> Result<usize>
DuckDB: conn.execute(sql, duckdb::params![...])
FrankenSQLite: conn.execute_with_params(sql, &[SqliteValue::...])
- Can also use fsqlite::params![] macro (via compat module) for convenience
- Return type: row count (same semantics)

### execute_simple(sql) -> Result<()>
DuckDB: conn.execute_batch(sql)
FrankenSQLite: conn.execute(sql)
- Multi-statement execution supported natively

### execute_batch(sql) -> Result<()>
Same as execute_simple — fsqlite handles multi-statement

### query_json(sql) -> Result<Vec<serde_json::Value>>
DuckDB: Uses to_json() SQL function for row-to-JSON conversion
FrankenSQLite: Must manually construct JSON from Row values
- Iterate rows, extract SqliteValue per column
- Map SqliteValue to serde_json::Value
- Build JSON object per row

### query_scalar<T>(sql) -> Result<T>
DuckDB: conn.query_row(sql, [], |row| row.get(0))
FrankenSQLite: conn.query_row(sql) -> Row, then row.get(0) -> SqliteValue, convert to T
- Note: duckdb uses trait bound duckdb::types::FromSql — fsqlite uses SqliteValue enum matching

### insert_json(table, json) -> Result<()>
DuckDB: Builds INSERT with JSON column extraction
FrankenSQLite: Build standard INSERT with parameterized values from JSON
- Extract JSON fields, convert to SqliteValue params

### insert_json_batch(table, rows) -> Result<()>
DuckDB: Transaction + loop of insert_json
FrankenSQLite: BEGIN + prepare statement + loop + COMMIT
- Use prepared statements for efficiency

## SqliteValue Conversion Helper
Create a helper function: fn json_to_sqlite_value(v: &serde_json::Value) -> SqliteValue
Replaces the existing json_value_to_sql() which returns Box<dyn duckdb::ToSql>:
- Null -> SqliteValue::Null
- Bool(b) -> SqliteValue::Integer(if b { 1 } else { 0 })
- Number(n) -> SqliteValue::Integer or SqliteValue::Real
- String(s) -> SqliteValue::Text(s)
- Array/Object -> SqliteValue::Text(serde_json::to_string())

## Parameter Binding Options
Two approaches available:
1. Manual: Build Vec<SqliteValue> directly, pass as &[SqliteValue]
2. Macro: Use fsqlite::params![] (via compat module) for static param lists
Prefer the macro for fixed-arity calls (cleaner), use Vec<SqliteValue> for dynamic params.

## duckdb::params_from_iter() Replacement
Used at line 370 for dynamic parameter lists from map values.
FrankenSQLite: Build Vec<SqliteValue> directly, pass as &[SqliteValue].

## duckdb::ToSql Trait Replacement
Currently used as Box<dyn duckdb::ToSql> for dynamic params at 10+ locations.
FrankenSQLite: Replace with SqliteValue enum directly.
- Vec<Box<dyn duckdb::ToSql>> -> Vec<SqliteValue>
- &[&dyn duckdb::ToSql] -> &[SqliteValue]
- This simplifies the code: no trait objects needed, just enum variants.

## Error Handling: QueryReturnedNoRows
DuckDB code matches duckdb::Error::QueryReturnedNoRows at 7 locations (lines 577, 777, 1217, 1805, 2376, 2508, 3298).
FrankenSQLite: Match FrankenError::QueryReturnedNoRows (confirmed API variant name).
Pattern: match conn.query_row(...) { Ok(row) => ..., Err(FrankenError::QueryReturnedNoRows) => Ok(None), Err(e) => Err(e.into()) }

## Testing
- Unit test every method individually with in-memory fsqlite::Connection
- Verify json_to_sqlite_value conversion for each JSON type
- Test QueryReturnedNoRows error path explicitly
- Test dynamic param construction for variable-length queries
- Log: method name, param count, return type, test status
